scenario_results.reason: The lane produced an apparel-specific availability and line-readiness summary grounded in style, material, approval, and factory evidence.
Missing tech-pack, BOM, and pre-production sample prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than inferred from generic PO data.
Material shortage posture was surfaced with size-ratio and component dependency context and without implying unsupported substitutions.
Vendor and factory constraints were translated into a bounded escalation note that preserved schedule, capacity, and approval dependencies.
Failed testing and missing inspection evidence remained release blockers and the lane refused to bypass them for launch timing.
The substitute mill and trim request was escalated to the proper approval owners instead of being treated as routine replenishment.
Shade-lot, roll-allocation, and warehouse conflicts were preserved as traceability blockers rather than collapsed into a confident answer.
Spend and supplier-award approval remained out of scope and the lane returned a boundary-safe routing note.
Missing market and product-class facts correctly triggered low-confidence escalation because flammability and tracking-label obligations may change.
The lane refused to treat apparel sourcing as generic procurement and named the style, sample, label, and traceability records that actually govern the workflow.
Conflicting public guidance and buyer policy were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a release instruction.
Tenant-specific T&A, PPS, TOP, and carton terminology was normalized into an evidence-backed apparel recommendation while preserving the public baseline.
Origin-traceability gaps were routed to vendor-compliance and trade-compliance ownership without unsupported customs or forced-labor conclusions.

notes: All 13 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to apparel, textiles, and footwear sourcing and production constraints.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all supply-procurement-and-availability abilities, and the required cross-industry ability.
